column1: # text = Comparing Clinton's and Trump's economic policies:
# text = It's all about the money Both Clinton and Trump want to invest in infrastructure and prevent the TTP trade agreement.
# text = But the two candidates for the US presidency disagree on taxes and energy policies, as well as on one fundamental issue.
# text = There is a fundamental philosophical reason for the differences between Donald Trump's and Hillary Clinton's economic policies.
# text = This is the role of the state.
# text = If in doubt, Trump wants the state's role to be minor, and warns of how the state can damage the economy with taxes and regulations.
# text = Clinton wants to lead the economy to its death and regulate it to its death, he says.
# text = He would do things totally differently, Trump says.
# text = He would repeal regulations, and lead a wide-reaching tax reform with low rates for private individuals and companies.
# text = Conversely, Clinton says that the state has particular tasks to fulfil, giving disadvantaged people opportunities - and it needs money to do so.
# text = This would increase taxes for the rich, she says.
# text = Interestingly, they have similar views on infrastructure.
# text = There has to be investment in infrastructure, Trump and Clinton say almost verbatim.
# text = The Democratic candidate has even announced the largest investment in jobs since the Second World War.
# text = State programs investing in infrastructure are classic Democrat policies, and are rejected by the tea party wing of the Republican party, which is critical of the state.
# text = Consequently, Trump could lead to discussions within the party.
# text = There are clearer differences to be seen in terms of energy and climate policies.
# text = Trump wants to build coal-fired power stations and bring coal production in the USA back up.
# text = Drilling for oil should be permitted in protected areas, and he wants to withdraw from the Paris Climate Change Treaty.

# text = On the other hand, Clinton wants to use her investment program to extend renewable energies, saying: "One country will become the superpower of renewable energies, and create millions of jobs."
# text = \"It will either be China, Germany or America - and I want it to be us.\"
# text = But it's not just infrastructure that sees the two candidates taking a rather similar stance - they agree in terms of international trade agreements as well.
# text = Neither want to sign TTP, the trans-Pacific trade agreement.
# text = At the same time, neither want to cut the USA off from international trade - they just want to protect American jobs better.
# text = They should then also be better paid, Clinton demands.
# text = The current minimum wage of 7.25 US dollars is a pittance, she says.
# text = She wants to raise it to 15 dollars an hour.
# text = It is not quite clear what Trump wants to do on this score:
# text = Once he said that the minimum wage had to be raised, then he said it should stay the same.
# text = But the big discussion of the months to come will likely revolve around both parties' tax policies.
# text = Trump is on one side, wanting to relieve the burden on the economy and citizens to boost growth.
# text = His critics state that this will just increase the budgetary deficit.
# text = Clinton is on the opposite side: as a minimum, she wants the rich to pay more taxes, and her critics say that more taxes are not the solution either.
